17: Elisha’s prayer — YHWH pᵉqaḥ nāʾ ʾet ʿênāyw wᵉyirʾeh, ‘YHWH, please open his eyes that he may see’ — and the servant’s vision of the mountain filled with horses and chariots of fire is the OT’s clearest narrative demonstration of the reality of the invisible. The servant’s natural eyes see the Aramean army; his opened eyes see YHWH’s army. The greater reality is invisible; what appears threatening is actually surrounded by what cannot be seen. 2 Corinthians 4:18 names the NT principle: mē skopountōn hēmōn ta blepomena alla ta mē blepomena; ta gar blepomena proskaira, ta de mē blepomena aiōnia — ‘we look not to the things that are seen but to the things that are unseen. For the things that are seen are transient, but the things that are unseen are eternal.’ Hebrews 11:1 defines faith precisely in terms of this structural dynamic: estin de pistis elpizomenōn hypostasis, pragmatōn elegchos ou blepomenōn — ‘faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.’ The servant’s opened eyes are the narrative form of what faith operates in: the greater reality that surrounds the threatening visible, and that prayer accesses. The apostolic prayer-pattern of Ephesians 1:18 (‘having the eyes of your hearts enlightened’) is the NT form of the same petition: open his eyes that he may see.
22: Elisha’s instruction to feed the captive Aramean army rather than kill them — śim lāhem leḥem ûmayim, ‘set bread and water before them’ — is one of the OT’s most striking enactments of the enemy-love principle. The king of Israel asks whether to kill; Elisha says: feed. The result (v23): ‘the Aramean raiders no more came into the land of Israel.’ The enemy fed becomes the peaceful neighbor. Paul in Romans 12:20 cites Proverbs 25:21-22: ean peinaō ho echthros sou, psōmize auton; ean dipsa, potizon auton — ‘if your enemy is hungry, feed him; if he is thirsty, give him something to drink; for by so doing you will heap burning coals on his head.’ The Elisha-feeding narrative is the OT’s enacted form of this principle: the enemy army given bread and water departs in peace. Jesus in Matthew 5:44 (‘love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you’) names the disposition; Elisha at Dothan provides the OT narrative illustration. The feeding of enemies at the prophet’s table also anticipates the kingdom-table of Luke 14:12-14, where Jesus instructs precisely not to invite friends but to invite those who cannot repay — the Elisha-table becomes a type of the eschatological banquet that reverses all expected social hierarchies.
1: The prophets' community asks Elisha for space to build by the Jordan: hannāqôm ʾăšer ʾănaḥnû yōšᵉḇîm šām lᵉpānêḵā ṣar mimmennu — “the place where we dwell before you is too small for us.” The expansion of the prophetic community signals blessing and growth under Elisha's ministry. The detail that the students build with their own hands points to a community of voluntary discipleship organized around the prophet — anticipating the pattern of itinerant teachers gathering disciples (cf. Jesus's disciples, Matthew 4:18-22).
2: nēlᵉḵāh nāʾ ʾel hayYardēn wᵉniqqᵉḥāh miššām ʾîš qôrāh ʾeḥāt wᵉnaʿăśeh šām māqôm lāšeḇet šām — let us go to the Jordan and each take a log and build a place to dwell. Elisha's agreement to accompany them (ʾĕlēḵ) models the leader-accompanies-disciples pattern. His presence transforms what would be ordinary labor into a scene of miraculous provision.
3: One of the prophets urges Elisha to come with them: hôʾel nāʾ ûlēḵ ʾet-ʿăḇādêḵā — please go with your servants. The community functions as disciples of a master (ʾăḇādîm, servants), not merely colleagues. Elisha consents: ʾēlēḵ — he goes. The master's willingness to be present at the disciples' ordinary work is characteristic of the servant-prophet pattern.
4: Elisha goes with them to the Jordan. As they are cutting wood, one of them loses the iron axe-head: wayyippol habbarzel ʾel-hammāyim — the iron fell into the water. The problem is compounded: wᵉhûʾ šāʾûl — and it was borrowed. The borrowed iron creates both a practical and ethical problem: the man cannot replace what is not his. The narrative's interest in the economic predicament of borrowed equipment grounds the miracle in everyday consequence.
5: The man cries out to Elisha: ʾăhāh ʾădōnî wᵉhûʾ šāʾûl — “Alas, my lord, and it was borrowed!” The double waw of distress in ʾăhāh (ah/alas) is the cry of someone caught between loss and obligation. The problem — a heavy iron tool sunk in the Jordan — is precisely the kind of irreversible situation (iron sinks, water flows) where only a miracle can restore what was lost. Elisha asks simply: ʾannāh nāpal — where did it fall?
6: Elisha cuts a stick (wayyiqaṣ ʿēṣ) and throws it at the spot where the iron fell — wayyišlaḵ šāmmāh wayyāṣep habbārzel — and the iron floated up (ṣûp, to swim/float). The miracle of making iron float inverts natural law: iron is among the heaviest common metals. Throwing wood (which floats) to cause iron to float creates a typological picture — some interpreters read the wood-thrown-into-water causing life from death as a type of the Cross (wood-in-water causing restoration). Elisha says: qah-lāḵ ʾōtô — take it up for yourself.
7: wayyišlaḥ yādô wayyiqqāḥēhû — the man stretched out his hand and took it. The miracle of the floating axe completes the Elisha miracle cycle (cf. 2 Kgs 2-5) showing the prophet's mastery over nature. The restoration of what was lost (the borrowed iron, returned to its owner) anticipates the broader redemptive theme of the Elisha narratives: the prophet restores what was taken — life, oil, food, and now a tool. Jesus as the greater prophet who restores what was lost (zētein kai sōzein to apolōlos, “to seek and save the lost,” Luke 19:10) stands in this lineage.
8: The narrative shifts to the Syro-Aramean military campaigns. The king of Aram instructs his commanders: bimqôm pelōnî ʾalmōnî taḥanōtî — “at such-and-such a place, encamp.” This is a placeholder formula (literally “so-and-so, thus-and-so”) for undisclosed locations. Elisha's foreknowledge of these secret positions is the theological pivot of the passage: the prophet has access to knowledge that belongs to the divine council.
9: Elisha sends word to the Israelite king: hišāmēr mēʿăḇōr hamāqôm hazzeh kî-šām ʾărām nōḥătîm — guard yourself from passing through this place, for Aram is going down there. The prophet functions as Israel's intelligence network — not through human espionage but through prophetic revelation. The pattern of God revealing hidden plans to his prophets (Amos 3:7: lōʾ yaʿăśeh ʾădōnāy YHWH dāḇār kî ʾim gālāh sôdô ʾel ʿăḇādāyw hannᵉḇiʾîm) is demonstrated practically here.
10: The king of Israel sends to the locations Elisha identifies — more than once or twice (lōʾ ʾaḥat wᵉlōʾ šetāyim) — and each time escapes. The repeated pattern of prophetic forewarning versus Aramean ambush suggests an ongoing surveillance-countermeasure dynamic. From the Aramean perspective, there is a leak in their security. The king of Aram suspects a traitor: mî mišellānû ʾel melek yiśrāʾēl — who of us is betraying us to the king of Israel?
11: The Aramean king's heart (lēb melek ʾărām) is troubled (wayyissāʿēr — stirred up, disturbed) over the intelligence failures. He suspects his own commanders. One of his servants answers: lōʾ ʾădōnî hammélek kî ʾĕlîšāʿ hannāḇîʾ ʾăšer bᵉyiśrāʾēl yaggîd lᵉmelek yiśrāʾēl ʾet hadᵉḇārîm ʾăšer tᵉdabbēr bᵉḥădar miškāḇêḵā — “None, my lord the king; but Elisha the prophet in Israel tells the king of Israel the words you speak in your bedroom.” The prophet hears what is spoken in secret chambers — a capacity attributed in the NT to Jesus: “Nothing is covered up that will not be revealed, or hidden that will not be known” (Luke 12:2).
12: The servant's report: Elisha the prophet tells Israel's king ʾet hadᵉḇārîm ʾăšer tᵉdabbēr bᵉḥădar miškāḇêḵā — the very words spoken in your bedroom. This claim of prophetic omniscience is remarkable in its scope — not just battle plans but private speech. The NT applies this same motif to Jesus, who knows what people think (John 2:25; Mark 2:8) and hears what is said in private. The Elisha narrative prepares an interpretive framework for this kind of prophetic/divine knowledge.
13: The king of Aram commands: lᵉḵû ûrᵉʾû ʾêkōh hûʾ wᵉʾešlaḥ wᵉʾeqqāḥēhû — go and see where he is, and I will send and seize him. The move from intelligence-sharing to active capture marks an escalation: the prophet is now a military target. Elisha is located at Dothan. The arrest-of-the-prophet pattern — authorities threatened by prophetic truth seeking to silence it — recurs throughout the OT (Jeremiah 38:4-13) and reaches its climax in the arrest of Jesus (Matthew 26:47-56).
14: The Aramean king sends horses, chariots, and a great army (sûsîm wᵉreḵeḇ wᵉḥayil kāḇēd) to surround Dothan by night. The military force deployed against a single prophet is a measure of how threatening prophetic truth-telling was to Aramean military power. The night siege creates the dramatic context for the servant's morning terror and the heavenly vision of v17 (present).
15: The servant rises early and sees the surrounding army: ḥayil sāḇîḇ hāʿîr wᵉsûs wāreḵeḇ — a great army surrounding the city, horses and chariots. wayyōʾmer ʾelāyw naʿar ʾăhāh ʾădōnî mah naʿăśeh — the servant cries, “Alas, my lord, what shall we do?” The servant's fear is entirely reasonable from the natural perspective. His inability to see the heavenly army represents the normal human condition: we perceive the physical threat but not the divine protection. Elisha's response establishes the fundamental theological principle of the scene.
16: wayyōʾmer ʾal-tîrāʾ kî rabîm ʾăšer ʾittānû mēʾăšer ʾittām — “Do not fear, for those who are with us are more than those who are with them.” This verse is among the most quoted in Christian theology for the concept of spiritual protection and divine accompaniment. The arithmetic is invisible to the servant: the more (rabîm) on YHWH's side exceeds the visible enemy. Paul's “if God is for us, who can be against us?” (Romans 8:31) is the NT equivalent. Jesus's promise of his presence with the disciples (Matthew 28:20) grounds the same reality in a permanent rather than episodic guarantee.
18: As the Arameans come down, Elisha prays: hak-nāʾ ʾet-haggôy hazzeh bassanwērîm — “Strike this nation with blindness.” The Hebrew sanwērîm (blinding, related to Genesis 19:11 at Sodom) denotes a mental-physical disorientation — struck unable to see clearly, confused. The prayer is answered immediately. This prophetic bestowal of blindness and sight parallels Elisha's earlier raising of the dead child (2 Kings 4): the prophet exercises authority over perception/death as divine instruments. The NT parallel is Paul's blinding on the Damascus road (Acts 9:3-9) — an encounter that simultaneously blinds physically and opens spiritual sight.
19: Elisha tells the blinded Arameans: lōʾ-zeh hadereḵ wᵉlōʾ-zōʾt hāʿîr lᵉḵû ʾaḥărāy — “This is not the way, and this is not the city; follow me, and I will lead you to the man you are seeking.” Elisha leads the men who came to capture him straight into the hands of the Israelite king — a reversal of captivity where the hunter becomes the captive without knowing it. The irony is profound: the prophet who was sought becomes the guide. Jesus, when soldiers come to arrest him in Gethsemane, also takes control of the encounter — “I am he” (John 18:5-6) causing the soldiers to fall back.
20: When they reach Samaria, Elisha prays again: YHWH pᵉqaḥ ʾet-ʿênê ʾēlleh wᵉyirʾû — “YHWH, open the eyes of these men, that they may see.” The same opening-of-eyes prayer that was prayed for the servant in v17 (that he might see the heavenly army) is now prayed for the enemy soldiers (that they might see where they are). Both blindness and sight are in the prophet's gift, through prayer. The enemies see themselves surrounded — in Samaria, Israel's capital. The contrast with the original plan (capture Elisha) and the outcome (the capturers are captured) illustrates the divine reversal theme central to Elisha's ministry.
21: The king of Israel asks Elisha: hāʾakkeh ʾakkeh ʾăḇî — “Shall I strike them down, my father?” The king's address of Elisha as ʾāḇî (my father) is the same address used by Elisha himself for Elijah at his translation (2 Kings 2:12). The prophet is the nation's spiritual father — the one whose guidance determines the nation's choices. The king is ready to execute the captured enemy soldiers; Elisha's answer will counter this impulse with a radical form of mercy.
23: Elisha instructs: śim leḥem wāmayim wᵉyōḵlû wᵉyišᵉtû wᵉyēlᵉḵû ʾel ʾădōnîhem — set bread and water before them that they may eat and drink, and go to their master. The king provides a great feast (kᵉrāh gᵉḏôlāh) and then releases them. wᵉlōʾ yāsᵉpû ʿôd gᵉdûdê ʾărām lāḇôʾ bᵉʾereṣ yiśrāʾēl — and the raiding parties of Aram no longer came into the land of Israel. The feeding and release of enemies produces peace. This is Proverbs 25:21-22 (“if your enemy is hungry, give him bread”) enacted in narrative — Paul quotes it in Romans 12:20 as the principle of overcoming evil with good. The morality of the episode prefigures the Sermon on the Mount's enemy-love ethic (Matthew 5:43-48).
24: Despite the peace won through mercy (v23), Ben-hadad of Aram later besieges Samaria — a new campaign suggesting either a different Ben-hadad or a later escalation. wayyiṣōr ʿal-Šomrôn — and besieged Samaria. The siege creates famine so severe that a donkey's head sold for eighty pieces of silver (šᵉmōnîm kesef) and dove's dung for five. The inverted economy of siege famine — worthless items (donkey's head) priced as luxuries — is a covenant-curse reversal: Deuteronomy 28:53-57 specifically threatens siege-induced cannibalism as the consequence of covenant violation.
25: The extreme famine prices: a donkey's head for eighty shekels, a qab (small measure) of dove's dung for five shekels. Donkey's head was non-kosher (Leviticus 11:4) — its consumption indicates the complete breakdown of dietary law observance under extreme hunger. Dove's dung is either literal (used as fuel or food) or a derogatory name for a cheap vegetable. The siege-famine sequence will serve as the backdrop for Elisha's impossible prophecy in 2 Kings 7 and its miraculous fulfillment.
26: A woman cries to the king for justice as he passes on the wall: hôšîʿāh ʾădōnî hammélek — “Save me, my lord the king!” The cry for royal salvation (yāšaʿ) during siege-famine creates the context for the cannibalism narrative. The king's helpless response — lōʾ yôšîʿēḵ YHWH mēʾayin ʾôšîʿēḵ — “YHWH has not saved you, from where shall I save you?” — acknowledges the theological dimension of the siege: it is YHWH's judgment, not merely Aramean military power.
27: The king asks: mah-lāḵ — what is your trouble? The formal inquiry before judgment follows legal procedure. The woman's complaint reveals the horror of siege-famine: an agreement to boil and eat each other's children in sequence. The cannibalism of children — specifically a mother-and-neighbor agreement to consume their own sons — is the exact fulfillment of the siege curse in Deuteronomy 28:53-57, where YHWH threatens that the most tender man and the most gentle woman will eat their own children in the distress of siege.
28: The woman's report: ʾimrāh lî ʾēt bᵉnēḵ wᵉnōḵlennû hayyôm ûʾet bᵉnî nōḵal māḥār — she said to me, give your son that we may eat him today, and tomorrow we will eat my son. The agreement represents the utter dissolution of the most fundamental human bond — maternal protection of children. The Deuteronomic curse is fulfilled literally. The narrative is placed precisely here — after the peace achieved through Elisha's mercy-feeding of enemies — to demonstrate that Israel's violence is directed inward when it should be outward in enemy-love.
29: wanᵉḇaššel ʾet bᵉnî wanōḵlēhû — so we boiled my son and ate him. The neighbor then hid her son (wayyaṣpᵉnēhû). The horror of the narrative is presented without editorial comment — the narrator records the worst outcome of siege-famine without softening. The king hears this and tears his garments in mourning (wayyiqraʿ ʾet-bᵉgādāyw). The rending of garments by the king connects this to prophetic mourning gestures throughout Kings and to the NT tearing of the temple veil (Matthew 27:51) — the garment of the old order torn in judgment.
30: As the king walks on the wall, the people see that sackcloth (śaq) is on his body beneath his garments — he has been in private mourning. The sight of the king in mourning garments publicly reveals the full depth of the national crisis. Kings publicly mourning signal the collapse of the protective order — the king cannot protect, cannot provide, cannot save. The inadequacy of human kingship in the face of covenantal judgment is the DH's running critique, answered only by the messianic king who actually saves.
31: The king vows: kōh-yaʿăśeh lî ʾĕlōhîm wᵉḵōh yôsîp ʾim yaʿĂmod rōʾš ʾĔlîšāʿ ben-Šāpāṭ ʿālāyw hayyôm — “may God do so to me and more also if the head of Elisha son of Shaphat stands on him today.” The king turns his grief into rage against the prophet — blaming Elisha for the siege. This is the classic pattern of shooting the messenger: the prophet who announces judgment is blamed for the judgment. Ahab tried the same with Elijah (1 Kings 18:17; 2 Kings 21:20). Jesus is similarly blamed for the consequences of his message.
32: Elisha is sitting in his house with elders when the king's messenger comes. He perceives the messenger coming before he arrives: hinnēh ʾăšer šālaḥ hammélek lahāsîr ʾet-rōʾšî — the king has sent to take his head. Elisha warns the elders: hold the door against the messenger, then press his foot behind the messenger. Elisha's foreknowledge of the assassination attempt (knowing the king's intent before the messenger arrives) parallels Jesus's foreknowledge of his own betrayal (John 13:21-27).
33: The king himself arrives after his messenger — wayyōʾmer mah-ʾîntîn laYHWH ʿôd — “This trouble is from YHWH; why should I wait for YHWH any longer?” The king's theological despair is the exact inverse of faith: he correctly identifies the source of the trouble (YHWH's judgment) but draws the wrong conclusion (abandonment of hope). Elisha's response in 2 Kings 7:1 will counter this despair with a specific, timed promise of abundance. The movement from “why wait for YHWH?” to miraculous provision within 24 hours is the narrative's theological point: the darkest moment precedes the dawn of salvation.
